data.title: H&R Reports Voting Results from 2026 Annual Meeting of Unitholders
data.description:
TORONTO, April 30, 2026 /CNW/ - H&R Real Estate Investment Trust ("H&R REIT" or the "REIT") is pleased to announce that each of the trustee nominees listed in the management information circular dated March 16, 2026 for the annual meeting of unitholders of the REIT (the "Circular") held earlier today was elected as a trustee of the REIT.

Each of the REIT trustees was re-elected with votes in favour ranging from approximately 98.97% to 92.44% of the votes cast at the meeting. Voting results for the individual trustees of the REIT are as follows:

 
Name of Nominee     Votes For    % Votes For  Votes Withheld  % Votes Withheld 
Lindsay Brand       158,656,125      98.54 %       2,350,712            1.46 % 
Mark M. Cowie       159,343,674      98.97 %       1,663,163            1.03 % 
S. Stephen Gross    158,643,525      98.53 %       2,363,312            1.47 % 
Brenna Haysom       148,834,324      92.44 %      12,172,513            7.56 % 
Thomas J. 
 Hofstedter         151,363,257      94.01 %       9,643,579            5.99 % 
Juli Morrow         159,285,567      98.93 %       1,721,270            1.07 % 
Marvin Rubner       159,163,043      98.85 %       1,843,794            1.15 %

In addition, the REIT is pleased to announce that a non-binding advisory resolution on the REIT's approach to executive compensation, as set out in the Circular, was passed as an ordinary resolution of the unitholders by a majority on a vote conducted by way of ballot. The results of the ballot were 151,249,540 votes (93.94%) voting in favour of the 'say on pay' resolution.

All other matters set out in the Circular were approved by the requisite majority of the unitholders at the meeting. Final results on all matters voted at the meeting will be filed shortly with the Canadian securities regulators.

About H&R REIT

H&R is one of Canada's largest real estate investment trusts. H&R has ownership interests in a Canadian and U.S. portfolio primarily comprised of high-quality residential (operating as Lantower Residential), industrial and office properties totalling approximately 21.2 million square feet.

Additional information regarding H&R REIT is available at www.hr-reit.com and on www.sedarplus.com.

SOURCE H&R Real Estate Investment Trust

/CONTACT:

Copyright CNW Group 2026

data.title: Boston Beer Q1 revenue falls 4.4% on volume declines
data.description:
Overview

  • U.S. craft brewer's Q1 revenue fell 4.4%, slightly missing analyst expectations

  • Adjusted EPS for Q1 missed consensus; gross margin improved year over year

  • Company repurchased $31 mln in shares during the quarter

Outlook

  • Boston Beer sees 2026 depletions and shipments down low- to mid-single digits

  • Company expects 2026 gross margin of 48% to 50%

  • Boston Beer projects 2026 non-GAAP EPS of $8.50 to $10.50

Result Drivers

  • VOLUME DECLINES - Co said Q1 depletions fell 4% and shipments fell 6.9%, mainly due to lower sales of Twisted Tea, Truly, Samuel Adams and Hard Mountain Dew, partially offset by gains in Sun Cruiser, Angry Orchard and Dogfish Head

  • PRICING AND MIX - Revenue decline was partially offset by price increases and favorable product mix

  • GROSS MARGIN GAINS - Gross margin rose 100 basis points, benefiting from price increases, favorable product mix, procurement savings, and improved brewery efficiencies, partially offset by inflationary, commodity and tariff costs

data.title: Consumers Are Still Spending, but Cracks Are Emerging — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Megan Leonhardt

Thursday brought a swath of economic data that, when pieced together, point to a softening consumer.

U.S. household spending remained solid during the first quarter, with 1.6% annualized growth, but that momentum has slowed in real terms over the past six months. That's likely due to a cooling labor market, slowing wage growth, and stronger pricing pressure — conditions that are not expected to shift much in the coming months.

Those factors will weigh on consumer spending, particularly discretionary outlays, over the next two to three months. That means economic growth could become more narrowly concentrated in the AI-buildout boom rather than consumer-driven for the remainder of the year.

Expect Americans to take fewer vacations or opt for smaller getaways closer to home as a result, Gus Faucher, PNC chief economist, tells Barron's. The financial cushion that many households experienced during the first three months of the year from higher tax refunds will start to fade by the summer, particularly if gasoline prices remain elevated.

The national average price for gas was $4.30 per gallon on Thursday, according to AAA. Gas prices have consistently averaged above $4 per gallon nationwide for the entire month of April. That has helped keep inflation elevated, with the personal consumption expenditures price index up 3.5% year over year in March.

Already, spending at restaurants pulled back in March, rising just 0.1% month over month, compared with a revised 0.5% increase in February. That's an area of discretionary spending where consumers tend to economize first, Faucher says.

Not only are pricing pressures weighing on households, but wage growth and savings rates are slowing as well. Real disposable income fell 0.1% — the second straight month of declines, according to data released by the Bureau of Economic Analysis on Thursday. The personal savings rate also dropped to 3.6%, its lowest level since October 2022.

"If consumers were compelled to live within their means, real consumer spending right now would be in a borderline recessionary state," writes veteran economist David Rosenberg, head of Rosenberg Research.

Average hourly earnings have largely been on a downward trend over the past year. Though some of the decline may be leveling off, it's unclear whether wage growth will be able to keep pace with inflation this year. The employment cost index picked up a bit in the first quarter, according to data released Thursday. But it was higher benefit costs, rather than a major uptick in pay, that drove the growth of 0.9% during the first quarter. Compared to a year ago, employers' labor costs increased 3.6% — a long way off from the peak of 5.1% in 2022.

Slower wage growth is being driven by the fact that there's more slack in the labor market than there was a few years ago. Unemployment remains low at 4.3%, but there also aren't as many workers available vying for jobs. That means employers face less pressure to raise wages to attract workers.

The slower real wage growth, paired with continued drawdown on savings, indicates that households are relying on savings to keep pace with rising costs. Moreover, without a big uptick in income growth, consumers may be forced to rely more on credit, as well as wealth effects from higher home values and stock market returns.

"We have spending growth outpacing income growth, that means that the personal saving rate is falling. And that's okay in the near term, but households can't continue to do that indefinitely. They're going to need to increase their savings, and that's going to weigh on spending growth through 2026," Faucher says.

As long as the labor market remains relatively stable, consumers should be able to hang on. And so far, employment conditions are not showing signs of faltering. The U.S. is still experiencing a period of slower hiring, especially compared to 2023 and 2024, with many businesses continuing to be more cautious with headcount. But layoff activity is low.

The number of Americans filing for initial unemployment benefits fell to 189,000 for the week ending April 25 — the lowest level since September 1969. While one week's worth of data should not be taken as gospel, weekly jobless claims have averaged 211,000 year to date, which is lower than in the same period than the last four years.

The low pace of jobless claims flies in the face of the high-profile layoff announcements at large tech companies that have received a lot of attention. "These are clearly not indicative of the broader reality. The overall pace of layoffs appears to have, if anything, slowed down from an already historically low pace," writes Stephen Stanley, chief U.S. economist at Santander.

The low unemployment will help keep households afloat, but expect some belt-tightening to start taking place by May — especially if price pressures remain elevated.

Write to Megan Leonhardt at megan.leonhardt@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Western Digital Corp Reveals Climb In Q3 Profit
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Western Digital Corp (WDC) reported a profit for its third quarter that Increases, from last year

The company's earnings came in at $3.17 billion, or $8.20 per share. This compares with $755 million, or $2.11 per share, last year.

Excluding items, Western Digital Corp reported adjusted earnings of $1.04 billion or $2.72 per share for the period.

The company's revenue for the period rose 45.4% to $3.33 billion from $2.29 billion last year.

Western Digital Corp earnings at a glance (GAAP) :

-Earnings: $3.17 Bln. vs. $755 Mln. last year.

-EPS: $8.20 vs. $2.11 last year.

-Revenue: $3.33 Bln vs. $2.29 Bln last year.

-Guidance:

Full year EPS guidance: $ 3.25 To $ 3.40

Full year revenue guidance: $ 3.650 B To $ 3.750 B

Result Drivers

  • AIG raises quarterly dividend to $0.50 per share, up 11% from prior dividend

  • Company says it remains on track to meet or exceed financial objectives set at March 2025 Investor Day

data.title: Casella Waste Q1 revenue beats estimates, raises 2026 outlook
data.description:
Overview

  • US solid waste services firm's Q1 revenue rose 9.6%, slightly beating analyst expectations

  • Q1 adjusted EPS and adjusted EBITDA both beat analyst expectations

  • Company raised 2026 guidance for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted free cash flow after acquisitions

Outlook

  • Company raised 2026 revenue guidance to $2.06 bln-$2.08 bln from $1.97 bln-$1.99 bln

Outlook

  • NewtekOne maintains 2026 EPS guidance range of $2.15-$2.55

  • Company targets 10% EPS growth for 2027, implying EPS of $2.40-$2.80

  • NewtekOne aims to originate $1.8 bln of loans in 2026

Result Drivers

  • DEPOSIT GROWTH - Co said strong growth in both business and consumer deposits supported balance sheet expansion

  • TECHNOLOGY-ENABLED PROCESS IMPROVEMENTS - Co said use of AI in origination, underwriting and closing reduced time and cost to close loans, enabling higher-quality credit origination

  • SHIFT TO BANK-FUNDED LOANS - Co said moving C&I LA Loan origination and funding to Newtek Bank improved funding costs and diversified loan portfolio

Analyst Coverage

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is 2 "strong buy" or "buy", 5 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell"

  • The average consensus recommendation for the corporate financial services peer group is "buy."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for NewtekOne Inc is $16.00, about 26.4% above its April 29 closing price of $12.66

  • The stock recently traded at 5 times the next 12-month earnings vs. a P/E of 5 three months ago

data.title: Atlassian Shares Shoot Up on Earnings Beat — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Anita Hamilton

Shares of workflow software firm Atlassian skyrocketed more than 20% in after-hours trading after it beat both earnings and revenue expectations for its third quarter ending on March 30.

This is breaking news. Check back for updates.
